expected.title: Household income × 10th-grade math
expected.blurb: The classic gradient: richer towns post far higher MCAS 10th-grade math pass rates. A clean, strong positive relationship that orients you to the map's main north-south spread.

expected.title: Low-income share × grade 3–8 reading
expected.blurb: Districts with more low-income students have markedly lower early-grade ELA proficiency — the single strongest poverty-to-outcome link in the data.

expected.title: Adults with degrees × 10th-grade English
expected.blurb: Where more adults hold a bachelor's degree, students score higher on 10th-grade ELA. Parental education tracks achievement almost as tightly as income.

expected.title: Income × four-year-college enrollment
expected.blurb: Wealthier districts send a much larger share of graduates straight to four-year colleges — opportunity compounds with income.

expected.title: Low income × chronic absenteeism
expected.blurb: Chronic absence rises steeply with the low-income share — poverty shows up as missed school days, not just lower test scores.

expected.title: Early reading × graduation rate
expected.blurb: Districts that get more kids reading proficiently by grade 8 graduate more of them on time. Early literacy is an early-warning signal you can see on the map.

expected.title: Educated towns × AP enrollment
expected.blurb: More college-educated communities push more juniors and seniors into AP/IB courses — a confirming signal of how expectations scale with adult education.

expected.title: Income × showing up to school
expected.blurb: Higher-income districts have higher daily attendance. A simple, intuitive pairing that makes the wealth gradient tangible.

surprising.title: Spending per pupil × math scores
surprising.blurb: Surprising: total per-pupil spending barely tracks 10th-grade math achievement. The highest-spending districts are NOT the highest-scoring — money and outcomes decouple, partly because high-need districts spend more by design.

surprising.title: Teacher pay × student growth
surprising.blurb: Counterintuitive: districts paying the highest average teacher salaries do not show faster student growth (SGP). Salary tracks local cost-of-living more than how much kids improve year to year.

surprising.title: Diversity × student growth
surprising.blurb: Worth a look: student racial diversity is essentially uncorrelated with academic growth. Diverse districts span the full range of growth — a useful counter to the assumption that demographics determine progress.

surprising.title: Commute time × math scores
surprising.blurb: Unexpected angle: longer average commutes weakly track higher math scores — a proxy for affluent, car-dependent suburbs that send parents far to work and post strong results.

surprising.title: Home values × discipline rate
surprising.blurb: Pricey-housing districts discipline a smaller share of students. Housing cost — a wealth proxy — maps onto who gets suspended, a sobering cross-domain pattern.

surprising.title: Students leaving × math scores
surprising.blurb: Districts losing more resident students to school choice tend to have lower math proficiency — families voting with their feet, visible as an outflow-vs-outcomes pattern on the map.

surprising.title: Counselor caseload × graduation
surprising.blurb: Surprising how weak it is: the number of students per guidance counselor barely predicts graduation rates. Staffing ratios alone don't tell the outcome story people expect.

surprising.title: Income × separate special-ed placement
surprising.blurb: Counterintuitive: wealthier districts place FEWER students with disabilities in substantially-separate settings. Lower-income districts lean on segregated placements more — inclusion tracks money, not need.

surprising.title: Class crowding × math scores
surprising.blurb: Surprising non-result: the number of students per teacher is essentially unrelated to 10th-grade math scores. Bigger classes don't mean worse outcomes here — a caution against reading the ratio as a quality signal.

surprising.title: Work-from-home × college-going
surprising.blurb: An odd but real proxy: districts with more remote workers send more grads to four-year colleges — remote work concentrates in the same educated, higher-income communities.
